coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescence probe molecularly imprinted polymer encapsulating N, S, B doped carbon nanodots from waste Clematis Chinensis Osbeck for sensing furazolidone
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Shujuan
Mao, Yanpeng
Song, Tinglu
Zhao, Xiqiang
Song, Zhanlong
Wang, Wenlong
coredata.dc:description: Preparing carbon nanodots (CNDs) from waste traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) can take full advantage of the abundant chemical compositions in waste TCM, reduce the quantities required for disposal and the processing costs. In this study, N, S, B doped CNDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method using waste Clematis Chinensis Osbeck, urea, thiourea, and boric acid as carbon, nitrogen, sulfur, and boron sources, respectively. N, S, B doped CNDs and red CNDs were encapsulated into molecular imprinted polymers (MIPs) to form a ratiometric fluorescence probe (CNDs@MIPs). By optimizing the preparation parameters (reaction temperature, reaction time and mass ratio), the relative quantum yield of N, S, B doped CNDs could be increased from 0.39% to 18.39%. The time-of-flight secondary ion mass spectrometry confirmed the elemental distribution of N, S and B and suggested the presence of active ingredients with anti-inflammatory and analgesic activities in the doped CNDs. The N, S, B doped CNDs could sense furazolidone (FZD) in a linear range of 5–700 mg/L with a detection limit (LOD) of 4.82 mg/L based on the mechanism of static quenching and the inner filter effect. The CNDs@MIPs probe could selectively sense FZD in the range of 5–400 mg/L with a LOD of 1.98 mg/L. In the different food samples, the recoveries of N, S, B doped CNDs and CNDs@MIPs were in the range of 90%–110%, indicating that the fluorescence sensors have excellent applicability for sensing FZD.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescence probe molecularly imprinted polymer encapsulating N, S, B doped carbon nanodots from waste Clematis Chinensis Osbeck for sensing furazolidone

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Preparing carbon nanodots (CNDs) from waste traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) can take full advantage of the abundant chemical compositions in waste TCM, reduce the quantities required for disposal and the processing costs. In this study, N, S, B doped CNDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method using waste Clematis Chinensis Osbeck, urea, thiourea, and boric acid as carbon, nitrogen, sulfur, and boron sources, respectively. N, S, B doped CNDs and red CNDs were encapsulated into molecular imprinted polymers (MIPs) to form a ratiometric fluorescence probe (CNDs@MIPs). By optimizing the preparation parameters (reaction temperature, reaction time and mass ratio), the relative quantum yield of N, S, B doped CNDs could be increased from 0.39% to 18.39%. The time-of-flight secondary ion mass spectrometry confirmed the elemental distribution of N, S and B and suggested the presence of active ingredients with anti-inflammatory and analgesic activities in the doped CNDs. The N, S, B doped CNDs could sense furazolidone (FZD) in a linear range of 5–700 mg/L with a detection limit (LOD) of 4.82 mg/L based on the mechanism of static quenching and the inner filter effect. The CNDs@MIPs probe could selectively sense FZD in the range of 5–400 mg/L with a LOD of 1.98 mg/L. In the different food samples, the recoveries of N, S, B doped CNDs and CNDs@MIPs were in the range of 90%–110%, indicating that the fluorescence sensors have excellent applicability for sensing FZD.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Encapsulating N, S, B doped CNDs

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, carbon nanodots (CNDs) as novel zero-dimensional nanomaterials, have been paid attention relying on the advantages of unique optical properties, excellent water solubility, extensive surface area, good biocompatibility and convenient surface modification. Many studies have been chiefly focused on preparation methodology and application filed, and the raw materials include chemical substances and natural substances such as orange peel [1,2], hair [3–5], kitchen waste [6–8], and traditional Chinese medicine (TCM). Owing to the peculiar properties, CNDs have been applied in catalysis, biomedicine, and chemical sensing. In the last filed, CNDs are served as sensor for wide range of analytes such as cations, anions, cells, bacteria, small moleculars and macromolecules based on various mechanisms. Antibiotics are commonly used to control bacteria and pathogenic microorganisms for plants, animals, and humans. They have saved many lives since their discovery in the early 20th century, however, drug fastness resistance has become a major health concern due to the abuse of antibiotics. World Health Organization reports about 5 million deaths due to antibiotic resistance every year worldwide. China is one of the largest consumers of antibiotics, and the demand has decreased since relative policies took effect; the yield of antibiotic was 218,000 tons and the demand was 138,000 tons in 2019. Furazolidone (FZD), a kind of broad spectrum antibiotics, is common nitrofuran antibiotic, to treat dysentery and gastrointestinal diseases [9,10]. But its metabolites have potential mutagenic and carcinogenic risks [11], European Union has prohibited FZD, nitrofural (Nif) and others to be applied in graziery and breeding industry since 1993, American and China also have done it respectively since 2002 and 2008 [12,13]. Strict maximal allowed limits of FZD have been implemented; therefore, it is significant to monitor FZD concentration constantly. At present, the common methods for detecting FZD include electrochemical method [14,15], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [16,17], liquid chromatography mass spectrometer (LC-MS) [18,19], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays [20] and fluorescence method [10]. Among these, electrochemical method have the shortcomings of tedious operations and poor selectivity. Although HPLC and LC-MS have higher sensitivity and lower detection limit (LOD), they process the drawbacks of expensive instruments, time-consuming and trained operators. Compared to the above technologies, fluorescence methods relying on the simple operation, high selectivity and sensitivity have been widely investigated.

China consumes approximately 70 million tons TCM [21], and generates 30 million tons of waste TCM each year [22]. Owing to the high recovery cost, waste TCM is generally backfilled, burned or stacked, which not only causes wasting of resources, but also maybe pollutes environment and does harm to people and animals. In virtue of the 50% extracted efficient of TCM [23], waste TCM still retain lots of nutrients such as crude fiber, crude protein and amino acids, as well as bioactive components such as polysaccharides and saponins. Enzymes and toxic proteins of waste TCM can been destroyed by thermochemical method to reduce the toxicity and alleviate drug property to enhance safety and reliability. Therefore, waste TCM have the advantages of low cost, easy access, low toxicity, and environmental friendliness. Moreover, with the prominent and serious problem of waste TCM discharge becoming a bottleneck restricting the development of the pharmaceutical industry, high-value treatment for waste TCM has become the development tendency [24]. Therefore, many researchers have proposed different feasible directions to raise added values. For example, it could be doped into animal feed [25–27], applied to organic fertilizer by composting technologies [21,28–30], used for wastewater treatment as an absorbent [31–33], or used to prepare the CNDs. CNDs prepared from waste TCM can ameliorate solubility, preserve the pharmacological properties of TCM, and even introduce new biological properties, which realize the best utilization of waste TCM. Shao et al. [34] prepared CNDs by hydrothermal treatment of Morus alba L. residues directly without any additional chemicals. Due to their low toxicity and rich surface functional groups, CNDs were not only applied for bioimaging in HepG2, but also carried lycorine as an anti-cancer drug to cancer cells. However, seldom reports reported CNDs from waste TCM, and there are urgent problems such as unstable quantum yield, uncontrollable size diameter and weak fluorescence, and lacking of the approaches of identifying active ingredients.

The relative quantum yield (RQY) of CNDs prepared from waste biomass is generally less than 10%, which is detrimental for sensing applications. Heteroatom doping is a common strategy to enhance RQY. The introduction of heteroatoms into CNDs can effectively tailor their surface defects, produce a new excitation energy level, and tune their electronic density and electron transfer, which are beneficial for enhancing their fluorescence quantum yield and improving their sensing performance. Nitrogen (N), sulfur (S) and boron (B) are the most popular heteroatoms in CNDs because they are neighboring atoms of carbon (C) with similar atomic diameters. Huang et al. [35] prepared N, S, B doped CNDs with an RQY of 8.9% from D-(+)-maltose monohydrate, boracic acid, and thiocarbamide by hydrothermal method. The obtained CNDs could sense sunset yellow selectively and sensitively with a LOD of 4.65 nM. Mohandoss et al. [36] achieved an RQY of 18.6% using blue-fluorescence N, S, B doped CNDs prepared via a hydrothermal method and the ratiometric sensor could detect Hg2+ selectively and sensitively by photoluminescence enhancement. All these results imply that the CNDs doped with multiple heteroatoms have great potential for sensing.

In this work, a novel strategy to prepare N, S, B doped CNDs from waste Clematis Chinensis Osbeck (CCO) was reported to realize the utilization of high additional value, and the reaction parameters were optimized to improve the quantum yield. Active ingredients with obvious anti-inflammatory and analgesic effects were characterized by time-of-flight secondary ion mass spectrometry (TOF-SIMS), which provided a feasible method to identify compounds in CNDs. N, S, B doped CNDs can sense FZD by static quenching and the inner filter effect (IFE) but with poor selectivity. Finally, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor, molecular imprinted polymers encapsulating blue-fluorescence N, S, B doped CNDs and red CNDs (RCNDs), named CNDs@MIPs, was formed for sensing FZD. These fluorescence sensors displayed good recoveries when sensing FZD in the different samples, suggesting that they have great anti-interference ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experiments
Apparatus
ce:section.ce:para: A scanning electron microscope (SEM, Zeiss Supra 55, Germany) was used to observe the morphology of samples under operating voltage of 5 kV, the sample was sputtered with gold for 240 s before observation. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai F20, USA) could clearly observe the morphology of the sample less than 0.2 μm, and the sample was coated on an ultrathin carbon film before observation. High resolution TEM (HRTEM) further was employed to characterize the lattice fringe, and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) was used to analyze the distance from the diffractive ring to the center to determine the type of crystal plane and the phase.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of samples were collected by powder X-ray diffractometer (Malvern Panalytical, Netherlands) at 40 kV operating voltage and 15 mA working current, with a scanning range of 10°–80° at a speed of 0.8°/min. The sample disk was rotated at 1 rps to obtain accurate diffraction data.

The surface functional groups were measured by Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometry (Thermo Nicolet 6700, USA). Due to dark color of samples, these and KBr was uniformly ground at a mass ratio of 1:300, then was made into a disc for further testing. The scanning range was 400–4000 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1 and 16 scans. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, USA) was collected using Al Kα ray under working voltage of 12 kV and filament current of 6 mA.

This work was under static mode of time of flight-secondary ion mass spectrometer (TOF-SIMS, PHI nano TOF II, Japan). The sample was sputtered using Bi3+ primary ion under 30 keV and 11.2 nA. The sputtering area was 0.1 μm × 0.1 μm with a speed of 10 nm/min and pixel resolution of 256 × 256 pixel. TOF-DR program analyzed the spectra and recognized the characterized ions. The secondary ion signal was color recorded forming 2D surface image, however secondary signal intensity could not ensure the absolute concentration for some elements because the ability of releasing secondary ions depended largely on the type of solid.

The absorbance spectra of the sample solutions were recorded by UV–visible spectrometer (MAPADA P5, China) in ultrapure water at a range of 200 nm–700 nm. The fluorescence spectra were measured in ultrapure water in ambient conditions by fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-4700, Japan). The experiment condition of measuring N, S, B doped CNDs was under excitation voltage of 400 V and slit width of 10 nm, the excitation wavelength was 344 nm with emission wavelength of 400–800 nm. While measuring RCNDs and CNDs@MIPs, the slit width was 5 nm and the operating voltage was 700 V.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ratiometric fluorescence probe molecularly imprinted polymer and its encapsulating
Preparation of N, S, B doped CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: Waste CCO was dried by drying oven at 65 °C for 24 h then ground until there was no particle agglomeration. The raw materials, including waste CCO powder, urea, thiourea and boric acid, were successively added to ultrapure water (60 mL) and stirred for 15 min. The solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon lined autoclave which was hydrothermally heated at 180–240 °C for 6–14 h. Afterward, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. The yellow solution was obtained by centrifuging at 10000 rpm for 20 min in order to remove large particles. The product was filtrated by 0.22 μm membrane filter, then the collected solution was further dialyzed with dialysis membrane (cut-off molecular weight = 1000 Da) for 24 h to remove small fluorescent molecules and unreacted substances. The dark brown solid, named N, S, B doped CNDs, was obtained by freezing drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CNDs@MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: The RCNDs were prepared by a solvothermal method according to original literature with certain modifications [37]. Briefly, 0.3 g neutral red and 0.3 g thiourea were dissolved into 60 mL ultrapure water under ultrasonication for 20 min. The product was transferred into a Teflon autoclave at 180 °C for 3h. The obtained dark solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 20 min and filtrated using 0.22 μm membrane filter, and then dialyzed by a dialysis membrane with cut-off molecular weight of 1000 Da for 24 h. The dark solid was obtained by freezing drying.

CNDs@MIPs were prepared by the suspension polymerization method according to the original literature [38]. Briefly, 50 mL ACN was put into a 250 mL brown three-neck flask, and then N, S, B doped CNDs and RCNDs were dissolved into ACN under ultrasonication. 0.2 mmol FZD and 1.2 mmol AMPS were put into the above flask, the forming mixture was stirred for 1 h to self-assemble between prepolymerized complexes. Then, 2–6 mmol EGDMA and 0.6 mmol AIBN were added when stirring and then the mixture was purged with argon for 30 min. Further, the brown three-necked flask was sealed and heated by hot block at 65 °C for 24 h. The obtained polymer was washed by ACN to remove uncombined polymers, and the mixture of acetic acid - methanol (1:9, v: v) was used to wash the polymer particles repeatedly under ultrasonication until no ultraviolet FZD signal, followed by centrifugation to remove organic solvent. Finally, the polymer particles were washed three times with ethanol and dried at 65 °C for 24 h in a vacuum oven. The non-imprinted polymers (CNDs@NIPs) was prepared by the same procedure in the absence of FZD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of FZD by N, S, B doped CNDs and CNDs@MIPs
Measurement of FZD in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: When measuring FZD by N, S, B doped CNDs, the fluorescence was measured under the conditions of the excitation wavelength at 344 nm. For detecting FZD, 4.0 mL N, S, B doped CNDs solution (0.1 mg/mL) was added to 100 μL different concentrations of FZD under the environment of Britton-Robinson (B–R) buffer (pH = 6.0) solution. The fluorescence intensity was measured after reaction time for 5 min. The fluorescence setting parameters were the excitation wavelength at 365 nm and 520 nm, and the excitation and emission slit width at 5 nm, and voltage of the photomultiplier tube at 700 V when sensing FZD by CNDs@MIPs. For detecting FZD, 4.0 mL CNDs@MIPs solution (0.5 mg/mL) was added to 100 μL different concentrations of FZD under the environment of B–R buffer (pH = 7.0) solution. The fluorescence intensity was measured after reaction time for 15 min at room environments. The control experiments of CNDs@NIPs were as the same as that of CNDs@MIPs.

To verify the selectivity of N, S, B doped CNDs and CNDs@MIPs towards FZD, the solution was added some possible interferences (Al3+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cr6+, Cr3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Fru, Tar, Glu, Asc, Lys, His, Gly, Cit, Suc, Nit, Nif, FZD), then fluorescence intensity was measured under the same conditions. First, N, S, B doped CNDs solutions (0.1 mg/mL) was dissolved in pH = 6.0 B–R solution, then 4.0 mL the above solution was added into 2.0 mM different interfering solutions, incubated for 3 min, and recorded the fluorescence intensity under the best excitation wavelength. Finally, 2.0 mM FZD solution was added to the mixture. The selectivity of CNDs@MIPs was tested using a similar method with some different parameters (concentration of CNDs@MIPs: 0.5 mg/mL, pH = 7.0, reaction time: 15 min, and concentration of FZD: 0.1 mM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of FZD in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Chicken feed was obtained from a local supermarket and treated by the essay [39]. In detail, the 15.0 g feed was extracted by 200 mL ethanol, then centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min. It was used to prepare various concentrations of FZD.

Honey treatment method was performed according to the literature [40]. The honey was purchased from a local supermarket. Frist, 50 mL honey was dissolved in 200 mL ethanol, sonicated for 20 min and shaken for 24 h. The supernatant was filtered and the product was for further using.

Pork was purchased from a local supermarket and processed as previously described [41]. Briefly, 5.0 g pork was extracted by 100 mL extract solution including ethanol, methanol and water (5:25:70, v: v: v). After ultrasonication for 30 min, the mixture was centrifuged at 8500 rpm for 30 min, and then the supernatant was used to synthesize solutions with different FZD concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Characterization of N, S, B doped CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Table S1, waste CCO included a quantity of C and O elements, while a few impurities, for instance, N and S elements accounted for less than 2%, suggesting that waste CCO contained less active ingredients. Therefore, the CNDs from waste CCO had a low quantum yield, and the addition of impurities was necessary. The effects of different dopants on the optical properties and sensing performance were studied. As shown in Fig. S1, Tables S2 and S3, co-doping with N, S and B significantly enhanced the RQY by tailoring the surface defects, tuning electronic structure and electron density, and generating new energy levels, which could basically improve the sensing performance. In the preparation of CNDs, urea, thiourea and boric acid were used as N, S, and B sources, respectively, and the reaction conditions were optimized by adjusting the reaction temperature, reaction time, and mass of the precursors. Fig. 1 showed that the RQYs of N, S, B doped CNDs (referring to the absolute quantum yield of quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4) were changing with the reaction conditions. It was seen that the RQY of N, S, B doped CNDs increased significantly in the range of 160–220 °C and then decreased slightly more than 220 °C (in Fig. 1a). Furthermore, the influence of reaction time on N, S, B doped CNDs was studied at 220 °C (Fig. 1b). Although the RQY always slowly increased with time increasing duration 10–14 h, the N, S, B doped CNDs were synthesized at 220 °C for 10 h considering the energy and time cost. The masses of urea, thiourea and boric acid played important roles in improving the fluorescence intensity of N, S, B doped CNDs (Fig. 1c). As shown in Fig. 1c, the optimal ratio of precursors was waste CCO: urea: thiourea: boric acid = 1.0: 0.5: 1.5: 1.0 (g). Eventually, the doped impurity elements was significant on improving the RQY and the RQY was increased from 0.39% (waste CCO only) to 18.39%.

As shown in the TEM images (Fig. 2 a), the prepared N, S, B doped CNDs were strongly monodispersed and uniformed with an average diameter of 5.37 ± 0.92 nm (Fig. 2b). The crystal structure of N, S, B doped CNDs was observed by HRTEM (bottom inset in Fig. 2a) with the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm [42,43]. And the N, S, B doped CNDs also had disordered carbon observed from SAED pattern (top inset Fig. 2a), which corresponded to a broad peak at 22.38° in the XRD pattern (Fig. 2c). The sharp peaks at 24.96° and 26.29° were corresponding to (100) and (002) planes of a graphite structure, respectively, which approved that N, S, B doped CNDs contained graphite structures. It was speculated that the root cause was the doping of the heteroelements. On the one hand, nitrogen doping promoted the forming of polycyclic fragments from waste CCO; on the other hand, heteroelements contributed to the orderly structure of CNDs and improved their crystallinity [44,45].

The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2d) characterized the surface function groups. Band at 3000–3500 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibrations of N–H and O–H [46], explaining the water solubility of the N, S, B doped CNDs. The peak at 2932 cm−1 showed the existence of the asymmetric stretching vibration of methylene [47,48]. The three absorption bands around 2063 cm−1, 1606 cm−1 and 1402 cm−1 were attributed to the NCS, CC/CO, and C–N functional groups, respectively [49,50]. The well-defined peaks were associated with the presence of N–B–O (1090 cm−1), B–N (783 cm−1) and C–S (700 cm−1) [49], furthermore, the absorption at 616 cm−1 was assigned to secondary amide [51]. These results confirmed the successful N, S and B doping in the CNDs.

XPS was employed to reveal the elemental composition and surface functional groups of the N, S, B doped CNDs. The full range XPS spectrum clearly showed peaks at 192.04 eV, 285.22 eV, 400.53 eV, 163.86 eV corresponding to B 1s, C 1s, N 1s and S 2p (Fig. S2), respectively, further verifying the successful doping of N, S, B elements into the N, S, B doped CNDs. C 1s high resolution spectrum showed the surface functional groups of N, S, B doped CNDs included NCS (288.34 eV), C–S/C–O (286.43 eV), C–N (285.52 eV), and C–C/CC (284.60 eV) as shown in Fig. 3 a [52]. The deconvolution of N 1s spectrum gave four sub-peaks at 401.24 eV, 399.96 eV, 398.97 eV, 397.85 eV which were attributed to N–H, N–C, N–B and pyrrolic N, respectively (Fig. 3b) [53,54]. The signal of B–O bond in B2O3 and N–B–O appeared at 193.64 eV and 192.45 eV, the signal of B–C bond was related to 191.76 eV, and the appearance of peak at 191.05 eV was ascribed to N–B (Fig. 3c) [35,55]. As shown in Fig. 3d, the peaks at 168.53 eV, 163.65 eV and 161.66 eV referred to CS, C–S–S 2p1/2 and C–S–S 2p3/2 in the S 2p spectrum, respectively [56,57]. The surface components of the N, S, B doped CNDs determined by XPS agreed with the FT-IR results.

The mass spectra and second-ion images obtained from TOF-SIMS revealed the elemental and chemical components. The second ion images (Fig. 4 e) directly reflected the distribution of N, S, and B elements of the N, S, B doped CNDs, homogeneously dispreading in the solid surface. Positive ions spectrum mainly included B+, CX + (X = 1, 2, 3, …), CXH+ (X = 1, 2, 3, …) and inorganic impurities, such as Na+ and K+ (Fig. 4a). Analysis on the N, S, B doped CNDs surface functionality utilizing the negative ion TOF-SIMS spectrum showed anion surface compositions revealed the existence of hydrophilic functional groups in the form of carbonyls, hydroxides and carbon based oxides (Fig. 4b) [58]. The active ingredients of CCO contained not only saponin compounds, but also flavonoids, lignans and some volatile oils, which played important roles in analgesic application. Almost all saponin compounds were extracted or decomposed at high temperatures, however, some flavonoids and lignans were retained in the N, S, B doped CNDs (Fig. 4c and 4d), providing the applicability as analgesic and anti-inflammatory agents. Considering the higher precision of TOF-SIMS and the complex compositions of CNDs, the content of these active ingredients might be lower. In summary, FT-IR, XPS and TOF-SIMS confirmed the successful doping of N, S, B into the CNDs, and their surface composed of amidogen, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups and some active ingredients of CCO. Considering that B could act as an electron acceptor for charge transfer, the N, S, B doped CNDs had excellent dispersibility in aqueous solutions without the need for further modifications.

The N, S, B doped CNDs solution displayed a yellow color under sun light and blue fluorescence after exposure to UV light as shown in the insets of Fig. 5 a. The absorbance peaks at 286 nm and 310–400 nm were related to π-π* transition of CC in sp2 cluster and n-π transition of CO, respectively (Fig. 5a) [59–61]. The fluorescence of the N, S, B doped CNDs was dependent on the excitation wavelength as shown in Fig. 5b and 5c. While the excitation wavelength increased from 300 nm to 450 nm, the center peak of the emission wavelength shifted from 400 nm to 500 nm (Fig. 5d), and the fluorescence intensity first increased and then declined. The highest intensity was achieved when the excitation wavelength was 344 nm (Fig. 5c), therefore it was selected as the excitation wavelength for the following FZD sensing test. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of the N, S, B doped CNDs solutions remained basically stable in different NaCl solutions (Fig. 5e), and could stand more than 85% of the original intensity after being stored in room environment for a month (Fig. 5f), which directly showed the N, S, B doped CNDs had the satisfied stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing FZD based on N, S, B doped CNDs
Optimization of sensing FZD based on N, S, B doped CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: The linear range and sensitivity are critical parameters for a fluorescence sensing system. It is well acknowledged that the concentration of the fluorescent substances has a remarkable influence on the quenching efficient. If the concentration of fluorescent material is too high, it is easy to reduce the sensing sensitivity [62], otherwise, its linear range is narrow simply which is adverse to analyze target analytes [63]. The concentration of N, S, B doped CNDs on the FZD sensing system was optimized using fluorescence intensity as an index. As shown in Fig. 6 a, fluorescence intensity increased at the beginning and then decreased while the increase of N, S, B doped CNDs concentration, and it evidently reached to the maximum when the concentration of N, S, B doped CNDs was 0.1 mg/mL which was optimum concentration in the following experiments.

The fluorescence intensities of the N, S, B doped CNDs in the B–R buffered solutions (pH = 3.0–11.0) were used to optimize the pH value. The fluorescence intensity was influenced patently by different pH solution (Fig. 6b). The intensity increased first then reduced with the pH, and reached the maximum at pH = 6.0. Therefore, pH = 6.0 was used for the following FZD sensing experiments.

To ensure that the response between N, S, B doped CNDs and FZD has finished, FZD solution with known concentration was added to the N, S, B doped CNDs solution, and fluorescence intensity was recorded by fluorescence time scanning. The results indicated that the response quickly finished within 1 min (Fig. 6c), thus fluorescence intensity was measured at 3min in the later sensing experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of N, S, B doped CNDs for FZD sensing
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence spectrum of the N, S, B doped CNDs was shown in Fig. 6d after adding different concentrations of FZD. With the increase of FZD concentration, fluorescence peaks decreased gradually and red shifted by approximately 20 nm. The absorption spectra of FZD overlapped with the excitation spectra of N, S, B doped CNDs, resulting in fluorescence quenching and a red shift. Fluorescence quenching can be described by the Stern-Volmer equation [64]:
[1]
F 0/F = 1+k q τ 0 C = 1+K sv C
where, F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the N, S, B doped CNDs in the absence and presence of the quencher, K q is the quenching rate constant, K sv is the static Stern-Volmer constant, C is the concentration of quencher. In this study, the quenching efficiency (F/F 0) displayed a great linear relationship with the FZD concentration in the range of 5–700 mg/L (R 2 = 0.9849), with a LOD of 4.82 mg/L (LOD = 3σ/k (n = 11), where σ was the standard deviation for the blank solution, and k was the slope of the calibration curve) (Fig. 6e). The analysis plot showed a linear trend with K sv = 5.848 × 107 M−1, and K q = 8.69 × 1015 M−1s−1. It was speculated that the quenched system was the main dominated contribution of static quenching given that K q exceeded the diffusion-controlled limit (1010 M−1s−1) [65–67].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of sensing FZD by N, S, B doped CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: Understanding the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CNDs is fundamental in order to exploit the fluorescence probe, identify the appropriate strategy and design the excellent preforming sensor. The quenching process is generally divided into static quenching and dynamic quenching according to the fluorescence lifetime. To explore the sensing mechanism of FZD, the average fluorescence lifetimes were measured as 0.73 ns and 6.70 ns, in the absence and presence of FZD, respectively (Fig. 7 a). The lifetimes were constant, confirming that the sensing mechanism was static quenching. After adding FZD to the N, S, B doped CNDs solutions as shown in Fig. 7b, the mixture showed an absorbance peak at 360 nm and the shapes of the absorbance spectrum was different from that of the N, S, B doped CNDs. It indicated that a new compound might generate from the interaction between FZD and N, S, B doped CNDs.

Additionally, the overlap between analyte absorbance peak and emission peak was observed (Fig. 7b). Therefore IFE existed in fluorescence quenching. Hence, it was inferred that those two interactions (static quenching and IFE) occurred during quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detecting FZD based on ratiometric fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: Although, N, S, B doped CNDs behaved relatively wide linear range and high sensitivity in the process of detecting FZD, N, S, B doped CNDs carried a large of hydrophilic functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which were prone to complex reaction with Fe3+ and Hg2+, resulting in the fluorescence quenching (Fig. 9c). Furthermore, chemical reagents with similar structures as FZD could significantly quench the fluorescence of N, S, B doped CNDs, reducing their selectivity. To avoid interference from other substances and improve the selectivity and reliability of detecting process, the ratiometric fluorescence probe (CNDs@MIPs) was prepared by encapsulating N, S, B doped CNDs and RCNDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of sensing FZD based on CNDs@MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: First, the amount of CNDs@MIPs for sensing system was studied. To ensure a wide linear range and high sensitivity, (F b-F 0b)/F 0b was calculated in this study, where F b was the fluorescence intensity of N, S, B doped CNDs and F r was representative of the fluorescence intensity of RCNDs. As shown in Fig. 8 a, when the CNDs@MIPs concentration was less than 0.5 mg/mL, quenching efficiency was such high that linear range was narrower and sensitivity was higher. On the contrary, fluorescence intensity ascended and linear range was wider, but quenching efficient descended. The experimental results indicated that the optimum concentration of CNDs@MIPs was 0.5 mg/mL, where a wide linear range and high sensitivity could be achieved simultaneously.

The acidity of the sensing system influences not only the optical properties, but also the charge between the fluorescent substance and the analyte. The fluorescence intensity was measured in the presence and absence of FZD under different pH conditions from 3.0 to 11.0. When the pH was more than 7.0, the red fluorescence peak had a blue shift, and the pink color changed to green under 365 nm light (insets in Fig. 8b). Considering the quenching efficient of the red fluorescence peak, the best pH value was 7.0 over the range of 3.0–11.0 (Fig. 8b).

To ensure a complete reaction of the response between the fluorescence probe and analyte, the mixture of CNDs@MIPs and FZD was detected by fluorescence time scanning. As shown in Fig. 8c, the results indicated that quenching efficient was unchanged within 15 min. Hence, signal readout was performed at 15 min. It was concluded that the imprinting layer on the MIPs provided fast mass speed resulting in a high absorbance capacity.

Moreover, the influence of environmental temperature on the detection system was explored. Due to the temperature differences in winter, reaction temperature was controlled by temblock from 20 °C to 70 °C. The test results indicated that the fluorescence intensity was not affected by the temperature (Fig. 8d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of CNDs@MIPs for FZD
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing performance of CNDs@MIPs was studied. With an increase of FZD concentration, the fluorescence intensity at 365 nm declined gradually, whereas that at 520 nm kept almost unchanged (Fig. 9a). As was observed in Fig. 9b, quenching efficient, F b/F 0b, displayed a good linear range with the concentration of FZD in the range of 5–400 mg/L (R 2 = 0.9851), where F 0b and F b were the fluorescence intensity of CNDs@MIPs at 365 nm in the absence and presence of FZD, respectively, and the LOD was estimated as 1.98 mg/L. Compared with other reported methods, CNDs@MIPs had a wider detection range (Table S4). The fluorescence response speed of CNDs@NIPs for FZD (Fig. S7) was apparently inferior to that of CNDs@MIPs. This phenomenon appeared mainly because CNDs@MIPs recognized specially FZD, however for CNDs@NIPs mainly relying on physical absorption or nonspecial absorption [68,69]. It was observed that the sensing rate of CNDs@MIPs was slightly slower than that of CNDs@NIPs owing to the reduced recombination of cavities and increased mass-transfer resistance [70].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of N, S, B doped CNDs and CNDs@MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the N, S, B doped CNDs and CNDs@MIPs (Fig. 9c) was evaluated by competitive binding tests. The fluorescence intensities of N, S, B doped CNDs hardly altered after adding common organic interferences, whereas they were quenched in the presence of Fe3+, Hg2+ and analog solutions. In the presence of Fe3+ and Hg2+, the fluorescence intensity of the CNDs@MIPs remained almost unchanged. Moreover, no fluorescence quenching of CNDs@MIPs was observed in the analogues, suggesting that it showed excellent specific selectivity for FZD because abundant three-dimensional cavities with specific sites were created for the rapid and selective recognition of FZD. Therefore, even the existence of interferences, CNDs@MIPs exhibited the satisfactory stability to specific absorption of FZD and achieved excellent fluorescence quenching effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real sample testing
Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, a novel strategy for preparing N, S, B doped CNDs from waste CCO was developed. Saponins were not found by TOF-SIMS in the N, S, B doped CNDs because of extraction and decomposition reactions, however, flavonoids and lignans for treating inflammation and analgesia were retained in the CNDs. The FZD sensing mechanism of N, S, B doped CNDs was static quenching and IFE. Common interferences seldom impacted the quenching process, but Fe3+, Hg2+ and the nitrofuran antibiotics disturbed fluorescence intensity evidently. Ratiometic fluorescence probe CNDs@MIPs, encapsulating blue-fluorescent N, S, B doped CNDs and red-fluorescent RCNDs, could sense FZD exclusively and accurately. Linear range of N, S, B doped CNDs and CNDs@MIPs were 5–700 mg/L, and 5–400 mg/L, their LOD were 4.82 mg/L and 1.98 mg/L, respectively, suggesting that they had a wider linear relationship and lower LOD. And in chicken feed, honey and pork, the recoveries of N, S, B doped CNDs and CNDs@MIPs were in 90%–110% range and the RSD were less than 3.7%, suggesting that the sensor probes had the excellent potentials in real samples and provided a good idea for sensing FZD.
